[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2025-09-12
[TITLE]: Northland Nature: Spider webs wait on foggy morning - Duluth News Tribune | News, weather, and sports from Duluth, Minnesota
[METADATA/INTRO]: The dawn is calm, 50 degrees and foggy. This is quite a change from the clear evening that we had last night. The weather service said this widespread fog would remain for the morning. I decide to take advantage of...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: A close look shows that a spider is in the midst of this apparent organized chaos. Such webs are often called "cobwebs." 
 In the leatherleaf plants of the swamp and on nearby spruces and tamaracks are many sheet webs. They appear to be bowl-shaped with another layer beneath, leading to the names of "bowl" and "doily" (cup and saucer). They are numerous in the swamp. The small spider that uses the web sits inverted beneath the top part, the bowl, and the lower, the doily, patiently waiting for insects to hit the top and drop down. 
 The fourth web type that I find on this walk is equally abundant. These are the circular webs known as orbs. Unlike the others, they are hung vertically on branches, grasses or other substrates. Also, unlike others, they are usually constructed anew each evening for a night of hunting.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: The Beauty of Spider’s Webs. | Lead up the Garden Path
[METADATA/INTRO]: The really lovely hot weather that we have been enjoying, has meant that on a couple of mornings we have got up to thick fog. Not to worry, this is the best time to see the beauty of spider’s webs...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: The really lovely hot weather that we have been enjoying, has meant that on a couple of mornings we have got up to thick fog. Not to worry, this is the best time to see the beauty of spider’s webs glistening in the sunlight when the sun eventually burns the fog away. Rather than just comment on the pictures I thought I would dig up a few facts, so all my information comes from Wikipedia and now I will share them with you, along with the photos I took a few mornings ago. 
 The word Cobweb is derived from a very old obsolete word – coppe – which meant spider, therefore coppe web = spider web.
